coredata.dc:title: Sensitive and selective determination of aloin with highly stable histidine-capped silver nanoclusters based on the inner filter effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Zhi-feng
Deng, Chen-hua
Wang, Jiao
Zuo, Yao
Wu, Jing-long
Wang, Xiao-peng
Lv, Tian-zhi
Wang, Yuan-yuan
Feng, Deng-yun
Zhao, Jie
Zhang, Cai-feng
Zhang, Ji-ming
coredata.dc:description: Aloin is a natural medicine, which possesses the functions of enhancing immune function, anti-tumour, detoxification, anti-bacterial, anti-stomach damage, liver protection and skin protection. Excessive use will cause side effects. Thus, sensitive aloin determination is very necessary in daily life. As far as we know, there are rare reports about aloin sensing through fluorescent methods. In this work, a simple method for the synthesis of fluorescence silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) was demonstrated by using histidine (His) as the templating agent and ascorbic acid as the reducer. The as-prepared silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs@His) possessed excellent water solubility and dispersion. Detailed research illustrated that the FL intensities of Ag NCs@His were linearly quenched in the presence of aloin (varying from 0.5 to 200 µM). The corresponding detection limit reached 0.052 µM. The static quenching and inner filter effect were the main fluorescence quenching mechanisms. This fluorescent sensor has been successfully employed for the detection of aloin in natural samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver nanoclusters
Histidine
Fluorescence determination
Aloin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing sensitivity of probe Ag NCs@His to aloin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV–vis spectrum, the excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of Ag NCs@His.
(A) The TEM image of Ag NCs@His; (B) The FTIR spectrum of Ag NCs@His; (C) The XPS spectrum of Ag NCs@His; (D) The XPS spectrum of Ag.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of aloin, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Ag NCs@His; (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag NCs@His with different concentrations of aloin (0–100 μM); (C) The Suppressed efficiency of observed (Eobsd) and corrected (Ecor) with different concentrations of aloin (0–100 μM).

(A) The UV–vis spectra of Ag NCs@His, aloin and Ag NCs@His+Aloin; (B) The fluorescence lifetimes of Ag NCs@His and Ag NCs@His+Aloin; (C) The calibration curve with aloin concentration from 0.5 to 30 μM.
The optimization of reaction time (A) and pH (B).
(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of Ag NCs@His with different concentration of aloin; (B) The calibration curve with aloin concentration from 0.5 to 200 μM.

The fluorescence emission spectra of Ag NCs@His with aloin or different ions (A) and substances (C) (the aloin concentration = 200 μM, the control substances = 2000 μM); Relative fluorescence intensities (F0/F) of Ag NCs@His solution with aloin or different ions (B) and substances (D).
The preparation of Ag NCs@His and its application.
The meaning of each symbol in the formula (3).

the absorbance at the optimal excitation wavelength
the absorbance at the optimal emission wavelength
the thickness of the excitation beam
the edge of the cuvette and the width of the cuvette

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Parameters used to calculate IFE of aloin on the of Ag NCs@His.
Comparison of different analytical methods for aloin determination.
The aloin recoveries in real samples.

head.ce:title: Sensitive and selective determination of aloin with highly stable histidine-capped silver nanoclusters based on the inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Humic Acid Engineering and Technology Research Center of Shanxi Province, Jinzhong 030619, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Humic Acid Engineering and Technology Research Center of Shanxi Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Humic Acid Engineering and Technology Research Center of Shanxi Province, Jinzhong 030619, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aloin is a natural medicine, which possesses the functions of enhancing immune function, anti-tumour, detoxification, anti-bacterial, anti-stomach damage, liver protection and skin protection. Excessive use will cause side effects. Thus, sensitive aloin determination is very necessary in daily life. As far as we know, there are rare reports about aloin sensing through fluorescent methods. In this work, a simple method for the synthesis of fluorescence silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) was demonstrated by using histidine (His) as the templating agent and ascorbic acid as the reducer. The as-prepared silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs@His) possessed excellent water solubility and dispersion. Detailed research illustrated that the FL intensities of Ag NCs@His were linearly quenched in the presence of aloin (varying from 0.5 to 200 µM). The corresponding detection limit reached 0.052 µM. The static quenching and inner filter effect were the main fluorescence quenching mechanisms. This fluorescent sensor has been successfully employed for the detection of aloin in natural samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Aloin (Fig. S1), as an anthraquinone compound, is concentrated \from the sap of leaves of monocotyledonous plant Liliaceae Aloe barbadensis Miller and Cape of Good Hope Aloe ferox Miller [1]. In recent years, aloin has attracted a great deal of attention because of its excellent characteristics, such as anti-tumour, detoxification, antibacterial, anti-stomach damage, liver protection and skin protection [2–4]. It can be applied for the treatment of constipation, infantile malnutrition, eczema, women's amenorrhea and hemorrhoids [5,6]. Meanwhile, it can inhibit and eliminate the development and reproduction of pathogens [7–9]. However, excessive use of aloin will cause some side effects (it could increase incidences of carcinomas and adenomas of the rat cecum and large intestine). Therefore, it is very necessary to establish more efficient methods for quantitative analysis of aloin. To date, some analysis methods, including gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) [10], ultra-performance liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) [11] and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [12], have been employed for the measurement of aloin. However, these reported detection methods suffers from various shortcomings like complex synthesis, high cost, lack of sensitivity and selectivity in aqueous medium, complicated pretreatment procedure and need of sophisticated instruments. Thus it is a difficult task to develop new detection methods towards aloin.

To the best of our knowledge, fluorescent analysis methods have gained intensive interest owing to their characteristics, such as low cost, strong operability and excellent selectivity and sensitivity, which have been employed in many areas, including drug sensing, cell imaging and catalysis [13–16]. The fluorescence detection is assumed to enhancement and quenching of fluorescence intensities, and an excellent linear range is always observed between FL intensity and concentrations of sensing substance [17,18]. The fluorescent probe is the core of fluorescent analysis methods [19,20]. Up to now, a series of fluorescent probes, such as organic dyes [21,22], quantum dots [23], carbon dots [24] and metal nanoclusters (M NCs) [25,26], have been widely developed and employed for the determination of pollutants. It is worth noting that M NCs, as a novel type of fluorescent nanosensors, have gained much more attention due to their excellent stability and water solubility, which are the guarantee for better applications in many fields [27–31]. For example, Guo et al. reported that the FL intensities of ascorbic acid stabilized Cu NCs could be selectively and sensitively quenched with the addition of tetracycline. The corresponding linear detection range was 0.9–70 μM and 80–150 μM. Meanwhile, the static quenching and inner filter effect (IFE) were confirmed as the detection mechanisms [32]. Lian et al. developed a fluorescence “turn-on” probe based on cysteine capped copper nanoclusters (Cys-Cu NCs) for the quinolones sensing. The fluorescent sensor has successfully been used to determine quinolones in tablets and human urine with satisfactory recoveries [33]. Zhao et al. group established a green-emission fluorescence probe based on glutathione-stabilized gold nanoclusters (GSH-Au NCs), which was exploited to detect Co2+ ion at a pH 6.0 [34]. Dong et al. synthesized lentinan stabilized platinum nanoclusters (Pt-LNT NCs) for the trace analysis of glucose with a wider linear range (5–1000 μM) and a lower detection limit (1.79 μM) [35]. Zhou et al. prepared polyethyleneimine-templated silver nanoclusters (PEI-Ag NCs) for the detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose [36]. As far as we know, histidine (His) is an amino acid and possesses some active functional groups (amino and carboxyl), which can improve the stability and water solubility of M NCs. Wang et al. designed His capped-copper nanoclusters for detection of protein kinase activity based on the fluorescence quenching [37]. Cai et al. developed a fluorescent probe using His-templated Cu NCs and realized the detection of tetracycline with detection limits of 0.047 μM [38]. To the best of our knowledge, there are rare reports about aloin sensing through fluorescent methods based on metal nanoclusters.

In this paper, for the first time, blue fluorescent silver nanoclusters were applied for the sensing of aloin, which was synthesized through the reduction of CuCl2 with ascorbic acid (AA) and His as the reducing agent and stabilizing agent at room temperature ( Scheme 1). The Ag NCs@His revealed blue fluorescence under UV lamp (The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were 385 and 468 nm, respectively.). The FL intensity of the Ag NCs@His was significantly quenched with the addition of aloin. Meanwhile, the mechanisms of fluorescence quenching were studied in detail through a series of characterization methods. Aloin, as a drug, is very likely to be present in the serum sample. Thus, bovine serum is used as actual sample to test the feasibility of Ag NCs@His. As far as we know, the Ag NCs was first used to determine aloin.

The F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and 2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) were applied to measure the fluorescence and UV–vis spectra data of Ag NCs@His, respectively. FT-IR data were obtained by using an FTIR-8400S Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) characterizations were performed on ESCALAB 250XI (United States). The TEM images were carried out on FEI Tecnai F30 (United States) with a voltage of 200 kV. The fluorescence lifetimes of Ag NCs@His with or without aloin were recorded on FLS-1000 combined fluorescence lifetime and steady state spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The fluorescence colour change of Ag NCs@His with aloin was observed by using ZF-1 three-purpose ultraviolet analyzer. The fluorescence pictures of Ag NCs@His were obtained by camera. The mixture were stirred through magnetic stirrer. The pH values of the mixture were recorded by using FE20 pH-meter (Mettler Toledo Instrument Inc., Shanghai, China).

All the compounds were directly employed without any purified. Silver nitrate (AgNO3, AR), L-Histidine (His, ≥99.0%) and ascorbic acid (AA, >99.0%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China. NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, CdCl2, CrCl3, HgCl2, CuCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, NiCl2, PbCl2, ZnCl2, glutathione (GSH), valine (Val), glycine (Gly), tryptophan (Trp), tyrosine (Tyr), cysteine (Cys), lysine (Lys), proline (Pro), aspartic acid (Asp), methionine (Met), glutamic acid (Glu), threonine (Thr) and aloin were analytically pure and purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Millipore Milli-Q was applied to produce Ultra-pure water (18.25 M cm−1).

All glassware was soaked in Aqua Regia solution (v(HCl)/v(HNO3) = 3:1) at room temperature for 24 h and simultaneously washed with ethanol and ultrapure water many times. The Ag NCs@His was prepared through the detailed steps below. Firstly, silver nitrate solution (500 μL, 0.005 M) was added into histidine solution (9 mL, 0.05 M) drop by drop under stirring. After 10 min of reaction, ascorbic acid solution (500 μL, 0.05 M) was slowly dropped into the above mixture at room temperature for 4 days. The color of the solution varied from colorless to light yellow. At the same time, blue fluorescence could be observed under ZF-1 three-purpose ultraviolet analyzer. The as-synthesized Ag NCs@His was purified through dialysis membrane (2000 Da) and stored 4 °C for further use.

For aloin analysis, in typical circumstances, 1000 μL of Ag NCs@His was dropped into 1000 μL of buffer solution (pH = 6.5) at stirring for 60 s. Afterwards, a series of amounts aloin were dropped into the above mixture, respectively. After incubation for 60 s at room temperature, the FL spectra and intensities were obtained through fluorescence spectrometer. For the selective test of aloin, some metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+) and amino acids (GSH, Val, Gly, Trp, Tyr, Cys, Lys, Pro, Asp, Met, Glu, Thr) were investigated under the same sensing conditions. The amount of aloin and other controls were 200 μM. Moreover, the interference tests were also studied.

Bovine serum samples were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., LTD. Black tea was purchased in the supermarket. The mother liquor of bovine serum samples were diluted by buffer solutions. To investigate the feasibility of the Ag NCs@His for aloin detection in bovine serum samples, the recovery experiments were studied. Different concentrations of aloin in diluted bovine serum solution and black tea were prepared and their fluorescence intensities were measured, then the corresponding recoveries were calculated through formula (1) [39]:
(1)
R
ecovery
=
measured
concentration
added
concentration
× 100 %

Quantum yield (QY) of the Ag NCs@His was obtained through reported methods [40,41]. The related data were measured under the same excitation wavelength and slit bandwidths. The formula is as follows (2).
(2)
Q Y =
I
I
R
×
A
R
A
×
η
2
η
R
2
×
QY
R
where QY is the quantum yield of Ag NCs@His, I refers to the integral area under FL spectra, η represents the refractive index of solvent, A refers to the UV–vis absorbance under the excitation wavelength. R is the reference (quinine sulfate).
A series of characterization methods, such as fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy, TEM, XPS and FT-IR, were applied to study the composition and structure of Ag NCs@His.

As seen in Fig. 1, the maximum value of excitation and emission wavelength were 385 and 468 nm, respectively. An obvious absorption peak around 400 nm was observed from the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Ag NCs@His [42,43]. The Ag NCs@His solution demonstrated pale-yellow and blue under the incandescent and UV lamp, respectively (the inset of Fig. 1). Meanwhile, the fluorescent emission and UV–vis absorbance spectra were compared between AgNO3, AA, His and Ag NCs@His. As shown in Fig. S2, there was an obvious fluorescence peak for Ag NCs@His. However, the fluorescent emission spectra of AgNO3, AA and His did not have any peak at the excitation wavelength of 385 nm. This phenomenon indicated that the fluorescence originated from Ag NCs@His instead of AgNO3, AA and His. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of AgNO3, AA, His and Ag NCs@His could be used to confirm the above results. As displayed in Fig. S3, an absorption peak around 400 nm for Ag NCs@His was only found. The above results suggested that Ag NCs@His was successfully synthesized through this simple strategy. Moreover, the influence of excitation wavelength for emission wavelength was studied varying from 360 to 395 nm. As seen in Fig. S4, as the excitation wavelength increased from 360 to 385 nm, the emission wavelength gradually increased. After increasing the excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength gradually decreased. The results indicated that Ag NCs@His possessed excitation-dependent properties, which was related to distribution of particle size or the different emission centers of Ag NCs@His [44–46]. Based on the formula (2), quantum yield (QY) of the Ag NCs@His was 5.9%.

The TEM analysis ( Fig. 2A) indicated that the Ag NCs@His possessed an excellent particle size distribution, and its morphology was spherical. According to statistics, the particle size was mainly distributed between 1.9 and 2.5 nm, and the average particle size was determined to be 2.25 nm. As seen in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2B), some functional groups are found on the surface of the Ag NCs@His. The transmission peak around 3425 and 3414 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration absorption peaks of NH. The transmission peaks around 3010 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration absorption peaks of -OH. The transmission peaks around 2886 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration absorption peaks of CH. The stretching vibration absorption peaks at 1635 and 1148 cm−1 were assumed to CO and CN, respectively. The bending vibration absorption peaks at 1568 and 1463 cm−1 were attributed to NH and CH, respectively. The FTIR spectrum showed that the histidine has been successfully applied to participate in the formation of the Ag NCs. Simultaneously, X-ray photoelectric spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to discuss the element composition and element status of the Ag NCs@His. Many characteristic peaks, including 287.0, 399.73, 531.05, and 370.73 eV, corresponded to C1s, N1s, O1s and Ag3d, respectively (Fig. 2C), indicating the existence of C, O, N and Ag element. Fig. 2D represented the state of the silver element. Two obvious peaks were observed at 367.90 and 373.92 eV, which were ascribed to Ag3d5/2 and Ag3d3/2, respectively. The results suggested that Ag (I) was successfully reduced to Ag (0) [47,48]. According to the above characterization results, the Ag NCs@His was successfully developed.
The fluorescence quenching mechanisms of Ag NCs@His towards aloin were studied by using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime.

The relationship was first discussed between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of aloin and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Ag NCs@His. As seen in Fig. 3A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of aloin obviously overlapped the excitation and emission spectra of Ag NCs@His, which was preliminary evidence of the existence of the inner filter effect (IFE) [49,50]. The Parker equation was further applied to confirm the existence of the IFE and shown as follows (3) [51,52]:
(3)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 −
10
− d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 −
10
− s
A
em

The meaning of each symbol in the formula (3) were listed in Table 1. Among them, Fcor/Fobsd values were calculated according to the formula (3) and listed in Table 2. Aex and Aem were obtained from UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag NCs@His with different aloin concentrations at the optimal excitation and emission wavelength, respectively (Fig. 3B). As seen in Fig. 3B and Table 2, with increasing the aloin concentrations, the Fcor/Fobsd, Aex and Aem values increased gradually. Moreover, the Eobsd and Ecor value increased as increasing aloin amounts, while Eobsd value was always greater than Ecor. The experimental results further proved that the fluorescent quenching mechanism was ascribed to the IFE [53,54].

Furthermore, the fluorescent quenching mechanisms were also studied through the static quenching and dynamic quenching. For this purpose, the interaction between Ag NCs@His and aloin was first investigated by using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. Compared to the UV–vis absorption spectra of aloin, Ag NCs@His and Ag NCs@His+aloin, a new absorption peak appeared in the UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag NCs@His+aloin ( Fig. 4A), which was ascribed to the formation of hydrogen bond between the -OH or -NH2 of Ag NCs@His and the -OH of aloin [55,56]. The above interaction leaded to the formation of non-fluorescent substances, which was initially attributed to the existence of static quenching mechanism [57,58]. The characterizations of fluorescence lifetimes were further employed to study our conjecture. The spectra of fluorescence lifetimes of Ag NCs@His and Ag NCs@His+aloin were illustrated in Fig. 4B. The average fluorescence lifetimes were calculated by using follows (4) [59]. The corresponding fluorescence lifetimes of Ag NCs@His and Ag NCs@His+aloin were 5.03 and 4.98 ns, respectively. The difference was only 0.05 between Ag NCs@His and Ag NCs@His+aloin, which was important evidence of static quenching instead of dynamic quenching [57,60]. To further certificate our preliminary conclusion, the Stern-Volmer Eq. (5) was applied for the study of the static quenching or dynamic quenching.
(4)
τ =
B
1
t
1
2
+
B
2
t
2
2
B
1
t
1
+
B
2
t
2
(5) F0/F=1+K SV[Q]=1+K qτ0[Q]

Where, F0 and F represents the FL intensities of Ag NCs@His and Ag NCs@His+aloin, respectively. K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. [Q] represents the aloin concentration (μM), τ0 refers to the FL lifetime of Ag NCs@His (5.03 ns). As shown in Fig. 4C, K SV and K q were equal to 1.15 × 104 L mol−1 and 2.29 × 1012 L mol−1 s−1, respectively. The K q value was much greater than 2 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1, indicating the existence of static quenching [61,62].

To verify the feasibility of the Ag NCs@His towards aloin sensing in actual samples, the influence of NaCl amount on the FL intensity of Ag NCs@His was studied at the excitation wavelength of 385 nm. As seen in Fig. S5, with increasing the NaCl concentrations, the FL intensities of the Ag NCs@His solution remained basically unchanged and no obvious enhancement or reduction of fluorescence appeared even if the NaCl concentrations reached 0.35 M. The experiments confirmed that this nanoprobe possessed excellent salt-resistant ability. The effect of storage time and UV light time on the stability of Ag NCs@His solution were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S6, the FL intensity of Ag NCs@His solution had slight change at room temperature for 20 d. Moreover, the ultraviolet radiation had little effect on fluorescence intensity of Ag NCs@His solution (Fig. S7). The stability experiments suggested that the as-prepared Ag NCs@His solution possessed intensive potential in biological and medical fields.

For aloin sensing, reaction time is an important factor, which has an intensive effect on the accuracy of detection methods. The fluorescent intensity of the Ag NCs@His in the presence of aloin (200 μM) was measured at 30 s interval and the data were illustrated Fig. 5A. The FL intensity of the mixture kept decreasing. After the reacting.

60 s, the FL intensity reached stable. After considering stability and time cost, 60 s was set as the optimal reaction time for further detection. Moreover, the influence of pH varying from 6.0 to 8.0on the sensing system was investigated. As seen in Fig. 5B, the change of fluorescence intensity was recorded. When the pH value was 6.5, the difference (F0-F) reached the maximum (F0 and F denoted the intensity at 468 nm without and with aloin respectively). Thus, 6.5 was set as the optimal pH value in the aloin detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing sensitivity of probe Ag NCs@His to aloin
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical characteristics of the developed platform were investigated under the optimal reaction time and pH value. As seen in Fig. 6A, with increasing the aloin concentration, the FL intensity at 468 nm decreased gradually. The FL intensity and the aloin concentration possessed an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.9974) (Fig. 6B). The linear regression equation was ln(F0/F) = 0.0094[C] + 0.022, and the corresponding detection limit was 0.052 μM (S/N = 3). Among them, F0 and F referred to the FL intensity of the developed platform in the absence and presence of aloin, respectively. Compared with previous reports ( Table 3), Ag NCs@His-based probes exhibited higher sensitivity and wider detection range than other methods.

To confirm whether this developed platform was designed for sensing of aloin, the selective and competitive tests were performed in the presence of different biological interference substances (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, GSH, Val, Gly, Trp, Tyr, Cys, Lys, Pro, Asp, Met, Glu and Thr). It could be seen significantly that the FL intensity of Ag NCs@His solution was effectively quenched with the addition of aloin, while others remained basically constant in the presence of the controls (from Fig. 7A to 7D). Additionally, the interference tests were also studied. The above controls were separately dropped into the Ag NCs@His solution with aloin, and the corresponding FL intensities.

were analyzed. As seen in Fig. S8, the effect of the mixture (solution of control and aloin) on the FL intensity of Ag NCs@His+aloin solution was negligible. These observed experimental data and phenomena suggested that the Ag NCs@His possessed a good selectivity for aloin over the controls, which indicated that the developed platform for aloin sensing had an excellent anti-interference ability.

To assess the practicality of this developed platform, the amounts of aloin in bovine serum samples and black tea samples were determined. As seen in Table 4, the satisfactory recoveries were obtained varying from 90.8% to 106.8% through detecting the concentration of aloin in bovine serum samples and black tea samples. Meanwhile, the relative standard deviation would not exceed 8.0%, which suggested that this developed platform was expected to analyze aloin in actual samples.

To the best of our knowledge, there are rare reports about aloin sensing through fluorescent methods. In this paper, for the first time, a fluorescent detection method for the aloin sensing was developed through chemical reduction, which was fabricated by using AgNO3 as the precursor, histidine as the templating agent and ascorbic acid as the reducer. The Ag NCs@His possessed excellent photostability and strong fluorescence. Interestingly, the fluorescence of Ag NCs@His was significantly quenched by aloin. Based on this phenomenon, a fluorescent sensing platform for aloin was established. The linear range was 0.5–200 μM between ln(F0/F) and the aloin concentration, and the detection limit could reach 0.052 μM. At the same time, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms were assumed to the static quenching and IFE. The satisfactory recoveries intimated that the Ag NCs@His had a bright future in many fields.

Zhi-feng Cai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Chen-hua Deng, Jiao Wang, Yao Zuo: English grammar correction. Jing-long Wu, Xiao-peng Wang, Tian-zhi Lv, Yuan-yuan Wang: measure fluorescence. Deng-yun Feng, Jie Zhao, Cai-feng Zhang: measure UV–visible spectra. Ji-ming Zhang: English grammar correction.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52002266), the Shanxi Applied Basic Research Project (No. 201801D121257), the Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths (No. 201801D221142), the Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2020L0499, 2019L0786) and the Fund for Shanxi “1331 Project” Collaborative Innovation Center (No. I018038) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.127224.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current status of aloe as a cure-all
Am. J. Pharm. Sci. Support Public Health

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aloe vera as a functional ingredient in foods
Crit. Rev. Food Sci. Nutr.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An evidence-based systematic review of Aloe vera by the natural standard research collaboration
Cardiovasc. Hematol. Agents Med. Chem.
